heroOverview: Jaundice is the yellow discolouration of the skin, sclerae, and mucous membranes caused by excess bilirubin accumulating in the bloodstream, a sign that the body is producing more bilirubin than it can process or excrete. Visible jaundice typically appears when serum bilirubin exceeds 2–3 mg/dL, roughly two to three times the normal upper limit. It is not a diagnosis but a clinical sign pointing to one of three categories of underlying cause: increased red blood cell breakdown (pre-hepatic), impaired liver processing (hepatic), or blocked bile drainage (post-hepatic). Worldwide, jaundice is among the most common reasons for emergency gastroenterology assessment. Identifying and treating the underlying cause promptly matters because several causes — biliary obstruction, acute liver failure, severe haemolysis — are time-sensitive and can become life-threatening if management is delayed.

keyStats.prevalence: Jaundice occurs in approximately 60% of term newborns in the first week of life; in adults it is the presenting sign in an estimated 1–2% of emergency medical admissions (WHO, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Neonates are most commonly affected physiologically; among adults, men over 50 have the highest incidence due to pancreatico-biliary malignancy and alcoholic liver disease
keyStats.avgAge: Adult-onset jaundice peaks in the fifth and sixth decades when biliary obstruction and liver disease are most prevalent
keyStats.globalCases: Viral hepatitis — one of the most common causes — affects more than 300 million people worldwide with chronic infection (WHO, 2024)

definition: Jaundice (ICD-10: R17) is a symptom-level diagnosis applied when bilirubin accumulates in body tissues, producing characteristic yellow pigmentation, before a definitive underlying aetiology has been established. Bilirubin is formed from the breakdown of haem, primarily from red blood cells; it is conjugated in the liver and excreted in bile. Elevated bilirubin is classified as unconjugated (indirect) — predominating in haemolysis and Gilbert's syndrome — or conjugated (direct) — predominating in hepatocellular damage and biliary obstruction. Pre-hepatic jaundice arises from excess bilirubin production overwhelming hepatic capacity. Hepatic jaundice reflects impaired uptake, conjugation, or secretion within the liver cells themselves, as in viral hepatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, cirrhosis, or drug-induced liver injury. Post-hepatic (obstructive or cholestatic) jaundice results from blocked bile flow in the intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary system, most often from gallstones or pancreatico-biliary malignancy. The unspecified code R17 is used while the workup is in progress; definitive care targets the specific underlying disorder. The condition spans general medicine, gastroenterology, hepatology, and in obstructive cases, general surgery or interventional endoscopy.

typesClassification.description: Caused by excess haemolysis producing more unconjugated bilirubin than the liver can conjugate; urine is normal in colour, stools are dark, and the liver and spleen may be enlarged. Haemolytic anaemia, sickle cell disease, and malaria are common causes.

Occurs when liver cells are damaged by viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or autoimmune disease and cannot conjugate or secrete bilirubin normally; both unconjugated and conjugated fractions may be raised, and transaminases are typically elevated.

Results from blocked outflow of conjugated bilirubin from the liver or bile ducts into the intestine, producing dark urine, pale stools, and often intense pruritus; common causes are gallstones and pancreatico-biliary tumours.

Occurs in most term newborns around day 2–4 of life due to immature hepatic conjugation of fetal haemoglobin breakdown products; it resolves within two weeks and is usually benign but requires monitoring for bilirubin levels that could cause kernicterus.

A specific hepatic subtype where medications — including antibiotics, statins, oral contraceptives, and antiepileptics — impair bile secretion or hepatocyte function, causing a cholestatic pattern that usually reverses on stopping the offending drug.

primarySymptoms: Yellow discolouration of the whites of the eyes (scleral icterus), which is often the earliest visible sign because bilirubin preferentially binds elastin-rich tissue.
Yellow tinting of the skin, starting at the face and spreading to the trunk and limbs as bilirubin levels rise above roughly 3 mg/dL.
Dark amber or cola-coloured urine caused by conjugated bilirubin spilling into the kidneys and being excreted in urine.
Pale, clay-coloured, or greasy stools resulting from reduced or absent bile entering the intestine, particularly in obstructive causes.
Generalised itching (pruritus), which can be severe in cholestatic jaundice and reflects bile salt deposition in the skin.
Fatigue and general malaise, reflecting either the underlying illness or reduced hepatic metabolic function.
Right upper quadrant or epigastric abdominal pain, which suggests gallstones, hepatitis, or liver capsule distension.
Nausea, vomiting, and reduced appetite, commonly accompanying hepatocellular causes such as viral or alcoholic hepatitis.
Fever and rigors, pointing toward an infective cause such as viral hepatitis, ascending cholangitis, or leptospirosis.
Weight loss, particularly when jaundice develops insidiously over weeks, raising concern for underlying malignancy.
earlyWarningSigns: Slight yellowing of the sclera noticed in natural light before any skin change becomes obvious.
Urine that turns noticeably darker over one to two days.
Mild right-sided upper abdominal aching or a sense of fullness after meals.
A few days of unusual fatigue and loss of appetite before skin discolouration appears.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of confusion, drowsiness, or personality change alongside jaundice, suggesting acute liver failure with encephalopathy.
Severe upper abdominal pain with fever and chills (Charcot's triad), indicating ascending cholangitis — a biliary tract emergency.
Vomiting blood or passing black, tarry stools, which can reflect varices or coagulopathy in advanced liver disease.
Very rapid worsening of jaundice over hours, particularly with fever and hypotension, pointing toward sepsis.
New jaundice in a pregnant woman at any stage, which requires same-day assessment to exclude intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy or HELLP syndrome.

causes.description: Hepatitis viruses infect and injure hepatocytes, impairing bilirubin uptake and conjugation; viral hepatitis A and E typically cause acute self-limiting jaundice, while B and C can cause both acute and chronic liver damage. Hepatitis A accounts for around 1.5 million symptomatic cases per year globally (WHO).

Stones migrating from the gallbladder into the common bile duct obstruct bile flow, causing conjugated bilirubin to reflux into the bloodstream. This obstructive pattern may be complicated by cholangitis or pancreatitis and affects an estimated 10–15% of adults in Western countries.

Chronic alcohol intake induces hepatic inflammation and fatty change, eventually causing alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis that impair all steps of bilirubin metabolism. Drug-induced liver injury from medications including amoxicillin-clavulanate, isoniazid, and NSAIDs can produce either a hepatocellular or cholestatic pattern.

Accelerated red blood cell destruction — from autoimmune haemolysis, hereditary spherocytosis, sickle cell disease, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, or malaria — floods the liver with unconjugated bilirubin faster than it can be processed, producing mild to moderate jaundice with anaemia.

Pancreatic head cancer and cholangiocarcinoma externally compress or infiltrate the biliary system, producing painless, progressive obstructive jaundice that is classically associated with a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign) and weight loss. About 70% of pancreatic cancers present with jaundice (NICE, 2018).

Severe systemic infections, including leptospirosis, yellow fever, and gram-negative sepsis, can cause jaundice through a combination of haemolysis, hepatocyte dysfunction, and intrahepatic cholestasis triggered by inflammatory cytokines — even without primary liver disease.

Oestrogen and progesterone metabolites impair canalicular bile transport in genetically predisposed women, causing pruritus and mild to moderate jaundice, usually in the third trimester; the condition resolves after delivery but raises the risk of stillbirth if severe.

riskFactors.description: Regular heavy alcohol intake is among the strongest modifiable risk factors for hepatocellular jaundice in adults; alcoholic hepatitis can cause acute decompensation even in people without established cirrhosis.
Chronic viral hepatitis is a leading preventable cause of cirrhosis and liver failure worldwide; vaccination covers hepatitis B, and direct-acting antivirals cure more than 95% of hepatitis C cases.

Overweight, female sex, age over 40, rapid weight loss, and high saturated fat intake all promote gallstone formation; stones in the bile duct cause obstructive jaundice requiring endoscopic or surgical removal.
Painless progressive jaundice in a person over 50 raises the probability of pancreatic or bile duct malignancy sufficiently that urgent imaging is mandated in most guidelines.

Hereditary conditions including sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia, and G6PD deficiency cause chronic or episodic haemolysis that results in recurrent unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia.

Many prescribed drugs, herbal remedies, and dietary supplements carry risk of drug-induced liver injury; regular review of medications is a practical preventive step, particularly with antibiotics, antifungals, and complementary therapies.
Hepatitis A, E, leptospirosis, yellow fever, and malaria are geographically concentrated; travellers without vaccination or prophylaxis are at elevated risk of acute infective jaundice.

Immature hepatic conjugation, bruising from birth, and polycythaemia of the newborn collectively produce physiological jaundice in up to 60% of term neonates, with phototherapy needed when bilirubin crosses treatment thresholds.

affectedDemographics: Newborns in the first week of life (physiological jaundice)
Adults aged 20–40 with viral hepatitis A or E in endemic settings
Adults aged 40–60 with gallstone disease, especially women
Adults over 50 with alcohol-related liver disease or biliary malignancy
People with inherited haemolytic disorders across all ages
Pregnant women in the third trimester (intrahepatic cholestasis)
Travellers returning from tropical regions (malaria, hepatitis E)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing jaundice begins with history and physical examination to classify the pattern as pre-hepatic, hepatic, or post-hepatic, which guides efficient investigation. Key historical points include speed of onset, pain, fever, drug and alcohol use, travel, blood transfusion history, and family history of liver or haematological disease. Physical examination looks for scleral icterus, skin stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema, leukonychia), hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and a palpable gallbladder. Blood tests are the first-line investigation: serum bilirubin fractionated into conjugated and unconjugated, liver transaminases (ALT, AST), alkaline phosphatase, gamma-GT, albumin, prothrombin time, and a full blood count. A predominant rise in unconjugated bilirubin with normal transaminases points toward haemolysis or Gilbert's syndrome; elevated transaminases suggest hepatocellular damage; a predominantly conjugated pattern with raised alkaline phosphatase points toward cholestasis or obstruction. Hepatitis serology (anti-HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HCV) should be sent when viral hepatitis is in the differential. Abdominal ultrasound is the standard first imaging test and reliably detects bile duct dilation, gallstones, liver texture changes, and masses. Where ultrasound is non-diagnostic and biliary obstruction remains suspected, magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) or CT of the abdomen provides detailed biliary anatomy. Liver biopsy is reserved for uncertain hepatocellular diagnoses such as autoimmune hepatitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies bilirubin elevation and distinguishes conjugated from unconjugated fractions to classify the jaundice type.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results are available within a few hours. Values above 2–3 mg/dL typically produce visible jaundice.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function panel (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, albumin, PT/INR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes whether the pattern is hepatocellular (raised transaminases) or cholestatic (raised ALP, GGT) and assesses liver synthetic function.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw; the combination of results provides the most important clue to cause before imaging.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and blood film
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies anaemia, reticulocytosis, and abnormal red cell morphology (spherocytes, sickle cells) that confirm haemolysis as the cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; the blood film is examined by a haematologist when haemolysis is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis serology (HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HCV)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses acute viral hepatitis A, B, and C, which together account for a substantial proportion of adult acute hepatocellular jaundice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results typically within 24 hours for most panels, though PCR viral load tests may take 48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to identify gallstones, biliary duct dilation indicating obstruction, liver size and echotexture, and space-occupying lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless 15–25 minute scan with a probe placed on the abdomen; fasting for four to six hours beforehand improves gallbladder visualisation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive detailed imaging of the biliary tree and pancreatic duct when ultrasound is inconclusive and obstructive jaundice is still suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–45 minute MRI scan; no radiation, no contrast injection is usually required, but the machine is noisy and confined.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Combines diagnosis and therapy — bile duct stones can be removed and biliary stents placed in the same procedure in patients with confirmed obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under sedation; an endoscope is passed through the mouth to the small bowel, and a catheter enters the bile duct. Recovery takes a few hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of jaundice is directed entirely at the underlying cause; there is no specific therapy for elevated bilirubin itself beyond treating the disease driving it. For viral hepatitis A and E, supportive care — rest, adequate hydration, and avoidance of alcohol and hepatotoxic drugs — is sufficient because these are usually self-limiting. Hepatitis B and C are treated with antiviral agents: nucleotide analogues for chronic hepatitis B and direct-acting antivirals achieving over 95% cure rates for hepatitis C. Alcoholic hepatitis causing jaundice is managed by abstinence, nutritional support, and, in selected severe cases, corticosteroids. Biliary obstruction from gallstones is managed by ERCP with stone extraction and sphincterotomy, followed by laparoscopic cholecystectomy; malignant obstruction is palliated with biliary stent placement and managed with oncological therapy. Haemolytic jaundice is treated according to cause — corticosteroids and immunosuppression for autoimmune haemolysis, hydroxyurea and transfusion protocols for sickle cell disease. Drug-induced jaundice requires immediate withdrawal of the offending agent with close monitoring of liver function. Neonatal jaundice above treatment thresholds is treated with phototherapy, which converts bilirubin to a water-soluble isomer for urinary excretion, or in severe cases, exchange transfusion. In all forms, monitoring for complications — coagulopathy, encephalopathy, acute kidney injury, and sepsis in severe hepatic disease — is essential.

medicalTreatments.name: Direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C
medicalTreatments.description: Combination regimens such as sofosbuvir-velpatasvir or glecaprevir-pibrentasvir achieve sustained virological response (functional cure) in over 95% of chronic hepatitis C patients within 8–12 weeks, resolving hepatitis-driven jaundice and halting disease progression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained virological response (cure) in over 95% of patients regardless of genotype per AASLD/EASL 2023 guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Nucleotide analogues for chronic hepatitis B (entecavir, tenofovir)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antiviral therapy suppresses hepatitis B viral replication, reduces liver inflammation, and prevents cirrhosis progression; most patients require long-term maintenance treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Greater than 90% rate of viral suppression to undetectable levels within one year (AASLD HBV guidance, 2018).

medicalTreatments.name: ERCP with stone extraction and biliary stenting
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic removal of common bile duct stones relieves obstruction promptly; in malignant obstruction, plastic or metal stents restore bile flow, relieve jaundice, and reduce pruritus pending oncological management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful biliary drainage achieved in approximately 90% of cases; stone clearance rates above 85% in a single session (ASGE guidelines).

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids for severe alcoholic hepatitis (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: In patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis — defined by a Maddrey Discriminant Function above 32 — a 28-day course of prednisolone 40 mg daily improves short-term survival in selected patients by suppressing hepatic inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 28-day mortality by approximately 15–20% in responders (Lille score-guided treatment); roughly 50% of patients show a response at day 7.

medicalTreatments.name: Phototherapy for neonatal jaundice
medicalTreatments.description: Blue-green wavelength light (wavelength 460–490 nm) photoisomerises unconjugated bilirubin in skin capillaries to a water-soluble form that can be excreted without conjugation; it is the primary treatment for physiological and pathological neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia above thresholds.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces total serum bilirubin by approximately 1–2 mg/dL per hour of treatment; prevents exchange transfusion in over 90% of neonates treated per AAP guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid for intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy
medicalTreatments.description: A bile acid that improves bile flow and reduces total bile acid levels, relieving pruritus and reducing fetal risk in cholestasis of pregnancy; treatment is continued until delivery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces total bile acids by approximately 50% and significantly decreases pruritus severity in randomised trials; fetal benefit is well established per RCOG guidance.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the gallbladder after ERCP stone clearance prevents recurrent gallstone-related biliary obstruction; performed as an elective procedure, typically within two to four weeks of ERCP.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complication rate below 2% in elective settings; definitively prevents gallstone-related jaundice recurrence in over 95% of patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of the pancreatic head, duodenum, and common bile duct for resectable pancreatic head or periampullary tumours causing obstructive jaundice; technically complex with five-year survival around 20–25% in node-negative disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year overall survival approximately 20–25% for resectable pancreatic cancer; perioperative mortality below 3% at high-volume centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Abstinence from alcohol
alternativeTreatments.description: For alcohol-related hepatic jaundice, complete abstinence is the most effective single intervention; liver function often improves markedly within four to eight weeks and can continue improving for months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hydration and rest (supportive care)
alternativeTreatments.description: In self-limiting viral hepatitis A and E, adequate fluid intake, rest, and avoidance of hepatotoxic substances including alcohol and unnecessary medications are the mainstay of care and produce full recovery in the great majority.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary fat reduction
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake lowers the risk of further gallstone formation and supports hepatic recovery in fatty liver disease, though it does not replace definitive treatment of existing obstruction.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist or hepatologist should be involved whenever jaundice persists beyond one to two weeks without a clear benign cause, when the pattern suggests biliary obstruction, when liver synthetic function is impaired, or when viral hepatitis B or C is confirmed. A haematologist is needed when haemolysis is identified. Oncological referral is urgent when imaging suggests pancreatic or biliary malignancy. Early specialist involvement establishes a definitive diagnosis, prevents complications, and guides therapy that cannot be optimised in a primary care setting alone.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hepatologist or gastroenterologist with access to an ERCP service and on-site abdominal ultrasound and cross-sectional imaging. For suspected malignant obstruction, a centre with a multidisciplinary hepato-pancreato-biliary team is important. If the underlying cause is haematological, co-management with a haematologist experienced in haemolytic disorders is valuable. Ask whether the practice has a rapid-access jaundice clinic, which can shorten the time to diagnosis for potentially time-sensitive causes.
preventionStrategies: Hepatitis A and B vaccination, which provides complete protection against two of the most common causes of infective jaundice.
Avoiding excessive and chronic alcohol intake, the leading preventable cause of hepatocellular jaundice in adults.
Maintaining a healthy body weight and reducing saturated fat intake to lower the risk of gallstone formation.
Reviewing all medications, including herbal remedies, with a clinician, particularly before starting drugs known to carry hepatotoxic risk.
Safe-sex practices and avoiding sharing needles to prevent hepatitis B and C transmission.
Pre-travel vaccination and antimalarial prophylaxis when visiting endemic regions.
Early antenatal screening for haematological disorders and hepatitis B carrier status.
lifestyleModifications: Abstain from alcohol completely during any episode of hepatic jaundice and reassess with a specialist before resuming any alcohol.
Eat small, low-fat meals to reduce biliary demand and nausea during recovery from hepatitis or biliary disease.
Stay well hydrated to support renal bilirubin excretion and prevent dehydration during febrile illness.
Avoid all potentially hepatotoxic medications and supplements unless explicitly approved by your doctor.
Rest adequately during acute hepatitis to allow liver regeneration, and return to activity gradually as symptoms improve.
Attend all scheduled follow-up blood tests to monitor resolution and catch complications early.
Keep a medication list including herbal and over-the-counter products for every clinical encounter.

recommended: Adequate caloric intake with high-quality protein (eggs, lean meat, legumes) to support liver regeneration
Plenty of water and non-caffeinated fluids to maintain hydration during febrile illness
Carbohydrate-rich meals in small portions when nausea limits intake
Fruit and vegetables providing antioxidants to support hepatic recovery
avoid: All alcohol during active jaundice and for at least three months after hepatitis resolution, or permanently in liver disease
High-fat or fried foods, which impair gallbladder function and worsen nausea in biliary disease
Paracetamol (acetaminophen) in doses above 2 g per day when liver function is impaired
Raw shellfish and untreated water in endemic settings, which are vehicles for hepatitis A and E

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate physical activity is beneficial for metabolic liver health and reduces the risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. During an acute episode of jaundice with significant fatigue or systemic illness, rest is advised and strenuous exercise should be avoided. As energy and appetite return, gradual resumption of walking and then moderate aerobic activity is appropriate. For those with established cirrhosis, exercise intensity should be discussed with the hepatologist, as sarcopenia is a key complication that regular supervised exercise can partially counteract.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antiviral or other hepatic medications daily without skipping doses.
Monitor urine and stool colour at home — persistent dark urine or pale stools during treatment should prompt contact with the clinical team.
Weigh daily if there is known liver disease, as sudden weight gain may signal fluid retention.
Keep a symptom diary including any new confusion, worsening jaundice, or abdominal swelling and bring it to appointments.
Attend follow-up liver function tests on schedule to verify improvement.
prognosis: The outlook for jaundice depends entirely on the underlying cause. Viral hepatitis A and E are self-limiting in the vast majority of immunocompetent adults, with full recovery expected within four to eight weeks and no chronic sequelae. Hepatitis B and C, when diagnosed and treated, carry excellent prognoses with modern antivirals — chronic hepatitis C is curable in over 95% of patients. Gallstone-related obstructive jaundice resolves promptly with biliary decompression in over 90% of cases. Drug-induced jaundice typically resolves within weeks to months of stopping the causative agent, though a small minority develop prolonged cholestasis. Alcoholic hepatitis causing jaundice carries significant short-term mortality — around 20–30% at three months in severe cases — and is heavily influenced by abstinence. Malignant biliary obstruction has a variable prognosis depending on resectability; pancreatic cancer carries five-year survival of around 20–25% when resectable and below 5% when metastatic. In all forms, early diagnosis and prompt management substantially improve outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: In viral hepatitis A or E, jaundice typically peaks in the first one to two weeks and resolves within four to eight weeks, with fatigue sometimes lingering for one to three months. Biliary obstruction from gallstones resolves within days to one to two weeks after successful ERCP stone extraction. Drug-induced cholestasis often improves over two to eight weeks after stopping the offending drug, though deep cholestasis can persist for up to six months. Alcoholic hepatitis requires four to eight weeks of abstinence for significant improvement in liver function tests; full recovery may take months. Neonatal jaundice resolves within one to two weeks with phototherapy. Malignant obstruction does not resolve without oncological treatment.
complications: Acute liver failure — rapid loss of hepatic synthetic function causing coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and multi-organ failure; rare but life-threatening.

Ascending cholangitis — bacterial infection of an obstructed biliary tract presenting with Charcot's triad (fever, pain, jaundice) and potentially progressing to septic shock if not decompressed urgently.
Hepatic encephalopathy — accumulation of ammonia and other toxins causing confusion, drowsiness, and coma, typically in chronic liver disease decompensated by jaundice.
Kernicterus in neonates — deposition of unconjugated bilirubin in the brain basal ganglia causing irreversible neurological damage if severe neonatal jaundice is untreated.
Cholangiocarcinoma or secondary biliary cirrhosis — rare long-term complications of prolonged untreated biliary obstruction.
Hepatorenal syndrome — kidney failure complicating severe hepatic jaundice in advanced cirrhosis, carrying high mortality without liver transplantation.

supportResources.name: British Liver Trust — Jaundice
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on the causes, signs, and management of jaundice from the UK's leading liver charity.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Jaundice
supportResources.description: Overview of jaundice types, diagnosis, and treatment with links to clinical resources from the U.S. National Library of Medicine.

supportResources.name: WHO — Hepatitis
supportResources.description: Global data and guidance on viral hepatitis prevention and treatment from the World Health Organization.

sources.title: Roche SP, Kobos R. Jaundice in the Adult Patient. American Family Physician, 2004
NICE NG12 — Suspected Cancer: Recognition and Referral (2023 update — pancreatic symptoms)
AASLD-IDSA HCV Guidance — Recommendations for Testing, Managing, and Treating Hepatitis C (2023)
AASLD Practice Guideline — Chronic Hepatitis B Update (2018)

Thursz M et al. Prednisolone or Pentoxifylline for Alcoholic Hepatitis. NEJM, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia without jaundice
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia from iron or B12 deficiency causes pallor and fatigue but not yellow sclerae; jaundice requires bilirubin accumulation, not just low haemoglobin. Haemolytic anaemia, however, can cause both simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria causes cyclical fever, sweating, and haemolytic jaundice together; in non-endemic countries it may be missed because the yellow discolouration is attributed to liver disease without considering the travel history.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis B is a specific viral cause of hepatocellular jaundice; unspecified jaundice R17 is the broader symptom code used before serology confirms the aetiology, though the clinical picture overlaps.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gallstones
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gallstones in the gallbladder alone do not cause jaundice; only when a stone migrates into the common bile duct does obstruction of bile flow produce jaundice — distinguishing asymptomatic cholelithiasis from choledocholithiasis requires imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: A common hepatocellular cause of jaundice; viral load and liver function tests guide antiviral treatment decisions.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic hepatitis C progressively damages hepatocytes and is a leading cause of cirrhosis-associated jaundice; now curable with direct-acting antivirals.

relatedConditions.name: Gallstones
relatedConditions.relevance: Migration of gallstones into the common bile duct is one of the most frequent surgical causes of obstructive jaundice.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Plasmodium falciparum malaria causes haemolytic jaundice and is the most common cause of acute jaundice in returned travellers from sub-Saharan Africa.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Leptospirosis causes severe hepatic and renal dysfunction with jaundice (Weil's disease) and must be considered in those with animal or water exposure.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Yellow fever directly names the jaundice it causes; vaccination is the definitive preventive measure in endemic areas.

faqs.question: What causes yellow eyes and skin?
faqs.answer: Yellow discolouration of the eyes and skin is caused by bilirubin, a yellow pigment produced when red blood cells break down. When the liver cannot process or excrete bilirubin fast enough — due to liver disease, a blocked bile duct, or excess red cell breakdown — it accumulates in the body and stains tissues yellow.

faqs.question: Is jaundice dangerous in adults?
faqs.answer: Jaundice in adults always needs medical assessment because it signals an underlying condition. Some causes — such as viral hepatitis A or drug reactions — are self-limiting. Others, including biliary obstruction and acute liver failure, are serious or life-threatening without prompt treatment. Jaundice combined with confusion, fever, or vomiting requires urgent care.

faqs.question: How is jaundice diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with blood tests measuring serum bilirubin and liver enzymes, which classify the type of jaundice. An abdominal ultrasound is the standard first imaging test to check for bile duct blockage or gallstones. Viral hepatitis serology and a full blood count are also routinely sent. Further imaging such as MRCP may be needed if the cause is not clear.

faqs.question: Can jaundice go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Jaundice from self-limiting viral hepatitis A or E, mild drug reactions, or Gilbert's syndrome often resolves without specific treatment within four to eight weeks once the underlying trigger resolves. However, obstructive jaundice from bile duct stones or tumours, and severe hepatitis, will not resolve without medical intervention. All new-onset jaundice in adults should be assessed promptly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between obstructive and hepatic jaundice?
faqs.answer: Obstructive jaundice is caused by a physical blockage of bile drainage — such as gallstones or a tumour — producing dark urine, pale stools, and itching. Hepatic jaundice results from liver cell damage, as in viral or alcoholic hepatitis, and is typically accompanied by raised transaminase enzymes. Blood tests and ultrasound distinguish the two.

faqs.question: Why does jaundice cause itching?
faqs.answer: Itching in jaundice, particularly in the obstructive type, occurs because bile salts that cannot be excreted into the intestine accumulate in the skin, where they irritate nerve fibres. The itch can be intense and is worse at night. Treatments include bile acid sequestrants such as cholestyramine, rifampicin, and relief of the underlying obstruction.

faqs.question: Can jaundice affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and is filtered by the kidneys, turning urine dark amber. In severe jaundice or hepatic failure, bile salts can cause renal vasoconstriction, contributing to hepatorenal syndrome — a serious complication of advanced liver disease. Dark urine that persists after jaundice clears should be investigated.

faqs.question: Is jaundice a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Painless, progressive jaundice developing over several weeks in an adult, particularly with weight loss, raises concern for pancreatic head cancer or bile duct cancer (cholangiocarcinoma). About 70% of pancreatic cancers present with jaundice. However, jaundice has many benign causes, and cancer is only one possibility — imaging and specialist assessment are needed to determine the cause.

faqs.question: What does dark urine mean in jaundice?
faqs.answer: Dark amber or tea-coloured urine in jaundice indicates that conjugated bilirubin — the water-soluble type produced in the liver — is being excreted by the kidneys rather than into bile. This points toward hepatic or obstructive jaundice rather than pure haemolysis, in which urine colour is normal. It is an important clue to the type of jaundice.

faqs.question: What is Gilbert's syndrome?
faqs.answer: Gilbert's syndrome is a benign inherited condition in which a reduced level of the UGT1A1 enzyme causes mildly elevated unconjugated bilirubin, producing intermittent mild jaundice — especially with fasting, stress, or illness. It affects around 5–10% of the population, causes no liver damage, and requires no treatment beyond reassurance and explanation.

faqs.question: How long does jaundice last in adults?
faqs.answer: Duration depends on the cause. Hepatitis A or E typically resolves within four to eight weeks. Drug-induced cholestasis usually clears within two to eight weeks of stopping the drug. Biliary obstruction from gallstones resolves within days to two weeks after stone removal. Alcoholic hepatitis may take months to improve. Malignant obstruction does not resolve without targeted treatment.

faqs.question: Can alcohol cause jaundice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic heavy alcohol use is one of the most common causes of jaundice in adults. Alcohol damages liver cells directly, causing alcoholic hepatitis and, over time, cirrhosis. Alcoholic hepatitis can produce significant jaundice within days of a binge in someone with established liver disease. Complete abstinence is the most effective treatment.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for jaundice?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if jaundice is accompanied by confusion or drowsiness, severe abdominal pain with fever and shaking chills, vomiting blood, or if you are pregnant. Any new-onset jaundice in an adult without an obvious benign explanation should be assessed within 24 to 48 hours. Do not wait to see if it resolves on its own.

faqs.question: What blood tests check for jaundice?
faqs.answer: The key blood tests are total serum bilirubin (split into direct conjugated and indirect unconjugated fractions), liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT), albumin, and prothrombin time. A full blood count looks for haemolysis. Hepatitis A, B, and C serology is added when infection is possible. Together these tests usually identify the category of jaundice.

faqs.question: Is jaundice contagious?
faqs.answer: Jaundice itself is not contagious — it is a symptom, not an infection. However, some causes of jaundice are infectious. Hepatitis A and E spread by contaminated food and water, hepatitis B and C by blood and sexual contact, and leptospirosis by exposure to contaminated water. Treating and isolating the infectious cause prevents spread; jaundice from gallstones, alcohol, or cancer carries no transmission risk.

faqs.question: What is the normal bilirubin level?
faqs.answer: Normal total serum bilirubin is 0.1–1.2 mg/dL (1.7–20.5 micromol/L) in adults. Visible jaundice usually appears at around 2–3 mg/dL. Direct (conjugated) bilirubin is normally below 0.3 mg/dL. Levels above 10 mg/dL suggest severe haemolysis, significant liver disease, or major biliary obstruction and warrant urgent investigation.

faqs.question: Can jaundice be prevented?
faqs.answer: Many causes are preventable. Hepatitis A and B vaccination protects against the two most common vaccine-preventable causes. Avoiding excess alcohol reduces hepatocellular jaundice risk. A healthy weight lowers gallstone risk. Safe food and water practices prevent hepatitis A and E while travelling. Prompt medication review prevents drug-induced jaundice.

faqs.question: Does jaundice cause pain?
faqs.answer: Not always. Painless jaundice with weight loss is a warning sign for pancreatic or bile duct cancer. Jaundice with severe right upper quadrant or epigastric pain suggests gallstone obstruction or acute hepatitis. Jaundice with mild diffuse abdominal discomfort and nausea is typical of viral or alcoholic hepatitis. Pain character and site are important clues to the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Can jaundice come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence depends on the cause. Gallstone-related jaundice can recur if the gallbladder is not removed after stone extraction. Alcoholic hepatitis recurs with resumed drinking. Viral hepatitis B may reactivate in immunosuppressed patients. Gilbert's syndrome causes intermittent mild jaundice throughout life but without progression or liver damage. Successful treatment of the underlying cause usually prevents recurrence.

faqs.question: What does pale stool mean in jaundice?
faqs.answer: Pale or clay-coloured stools indicate that bile is not reaching the intestine, which is a hallmark of obstructive jaundice. Bile gives stool its normal brown colour. Obstruction from gallstones, a pancreatic tumour, or bile duct disease prevents bile from draining into the gut, producing both pale stools and dark urine simultaneously — a pair of signs that reliably points to post-hepatic obstruction.

metaDescription: Jaundice turns skin and eyes yellow when bilirubin builds up in the blood. Learn the causes, diagnosis tests, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed guide.
